coredata.dc:title: Ag@CDs nanohybrid: Fabrication, design of a multi-mode chemosensory probe for selective Fe3+ detection and logic gate operation
coredata.dc:creator: Ajith, M.P.
Pardhiya, Sonali
Prabhakar, Arun Kumar
Rajamani, Paulraj
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we propose a unique use of silver–carbon dot nanohybrid (Ag@CDs) with an average size of 16 nm as a multi-mode sensor for the selective detection of Fe3+ and the construction of logic gates based on these unique detection properties. The Ag NPs exhibit colourimetric sensing and fluorescence quenching in response to Fe3+ in the concentration range of 10–100 ppm, with the carbon dots acting as the fluorescent entity. Surprisingly, the nanohybrid was shown to have excellent sensitivity to Fe3+, resulting in aggregation and formation of yellowish-brown clumps. When the reaction mixture was treated with Fe3+, there was a discernible change in the colour of the assay mixture and fluorescence quenching. That is, the Ag@CDs acted as a calorimetric and fluorescence multi-mode sensor. Even in interfering groups in the natural river water sample, the produced nanohybrid displayed good selectivity towards Fe3+, with a minimum LOD of 0.76 ppm. Further, we constructed an advanced logic system, IMP-OR gate, by using additional inputs - ascorbic acid and urea.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Ag NPs
Ag@CDs
Heavy metal sensors
Logic operations

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Improved Carbon-substrate nanomaterials for treatment and detection of hazardous pollutants in the water/wastewater Edited by Alireza Khataee, Yasser Vasseghian, and Amir Razmjou

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and Ag NPs
Detection of Fe3+ and logic gate construction
Effect of pH in fluorescence of Ag@CDs
Application of Ag@CDs in real environmental samples as sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thankfully financial assistance is acknowledged for the INSPIRE Senior Research Fellowship from the Department of Science and Technology , New Delhi, India, to AMP. We also thankfully acknowledge Mr Mohamed Nabeel Mattath from the School of Chemical Science & Engineering, Tongji University , Shanghai, P.R China, for helping with logic gate fabrication.

Thankfully financial assistance is acknowledged for the INSPIRE Senior Research Fellowship from the Department of Science and Technology, New Delhi, India, to AMP. We also thankfully acknowledge Mr Mohamed Nabeel Mattath from the School of Chemical Science & Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, P.R China, for helping with logic gate fabrication.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization. a. UV–visible absorption spectra of carbon dots (CDs), silver nanoparticle (Ag NPs), and Ag-CDs nanohybrid (Ag@CDs). Inset. Image showing colour of CDs, Ag NPs, and Ag@CDs. b. The hydrodynamic diameter of Ag NPs. c. TEM image of Ag NPs. d. The hydrodynamic diameter of Ag@CDs. e. TEM image of Ag@CDs. f. HR-TEM image of Ag@CDs showing the amorphous layer of CDs deposited on the crystalline AG NPs with lattice spacing 0.261 nm. g. FTIR spectra of Ag NPS and Ag@CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Selectivity of Ag@CDs towards analytes. a. Colour change in the reaction mixture. b. Fluorescence spectra on the reaction of Ag@CDs with analytes. c. Fluorescence response of Ag@CDs on treatment with analytes (F0 and F is the fluorescence in the absence and presence of analytes, respectively) d. Change in hydrodynamic of Ag@CDs on treatment with analytes. e. Schematic illustration representing the mechanism behind the detection of Fe3+ by Ag@CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Sensitivity of Ag@CDs towards Fe3+. a. UV–Visible absorption spectra of Ag@CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 0–100 ppm. b. Fluorescence emission spectra of Ag@CDs on the addition of different Fe3+ concentrations in 0–100 ppm. c. Calibration plot between Fe3+concentrations and Ag@CDs fluorescence intensity.
a. Effect of pH on the fluorescence of Ag@CDs b. Feasibility of Ag@CDs to detect Fe3+ from tap water and Yamuna river water.

Logic operations using Ag@CDs. a. Truth table for different input combinations (IC) b. Fluorescence response of Ag@CDs under different input combination (Relative fluorescence 0.3 is set as threshold) c. Logic Symbol for 3 input IMPLICATION-OR gate.
Average Zeta potential when Ag@CDs are treated with different analytes.
Comparison of the developed probe with previously reported nano-sensors for Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Ag@CDs nanohybrid: Fabrication, design of a multi-mode chemosensory probe for selective Fe3+ detection and logic gate operation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi-110067, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NUS Environmental Research Institute, National University of Singapore, 1 Create Way, Create Tower #15-02, 138602, Singapore
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: NUS Environmental Research Institute, National University of Singapore, 1 create way, Create Tower #15-02, 138602, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we propose a unique use of silver–carbon dot nanohybrid (Ag@CDs) with an average size of 16 nm as a multi-mode sensor for the selective detection of Fe3+ and the construction of logic gates based on these unique detection properties. The Ag NPs exhibit colourimetric sensing and fluorescence quenching in response to Fe3+ in the concentration range of 10–100 ppm, with the carbon dots acting as the fluorescent entity. Surprisingly, the nanohybrid was shown to have excellent sensitivity to Fe3+, resulting in aggregation and formation of yellowish-brown clumps. When the reaction mixture was treated with Fe3+, there was a discernible change in the colour of the assay mixture and fluorescence quenching. That is, the Ag@CDs acted as a calorimetric and fluorescence multi-mode sensor. Even in interfering groups in the natural river water sample, the produced nanohybrid displayed good selectivity towards Fe3+, with a minimum LOD of 0.76 ppm. Further, we constructed an advanced logic system, IMP-OR gate, by using additional inputs - ascorbic acid and urea.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are naturally formed elements with a high atomic weight and a density of at least five times that of water. In recent years, environmental contamination because of these metals has been a rising ecological and public health problem (Ajith and James, 2016). Due to an exponential growth in their use in a range of industrial, agricultural, residential, and technological applications, human exposure has also increased significantly (Sharma et al., 2021). Heavy metals reach the environment from geogenic, industrial, agricultural, medicinal, and domestic sources. Mainly, mining, foundries and smelters, and other metal-based industrial activities are critical heavy metal polluters of the environment (He et al., 2005).

Due to their potentially devastating influence on humans, enormous efforts have been committed in recent years to detect and quantify heavy metals in environmental and biological contexts. Iron is the most prevalent heavy metal in the human body. It plays a vital role in physiological processes such as oxygen transport, a cofactor in enzyme catalysis, structural compounds in proteins such as myoglobin, and energy production (Hirayama and Nagasawa, 2017). Excess iron in the human body may cause significant disorders such as toxicity in organs, leading to cancer, neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimer's and Parkinson's, irregular heartbeat, and cirrhosis of the liver (Li and Reichmann, 2016; Torti and Torti, 2013). Fe may reach the human body via polluted water from industrial and consumer waste or heavy metals released from soil breakdown caused by acidic rain. Considering potential consequences, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) considers Fe a vital trace contaminant and establishes an acceptable level of Fe in drinking water at 0.3 ppm. In human blood, its concentration ranges from 60 to 170 μg per deciliter (mcg/dL).

There is a tremendous interest in developing novel nanomaterials and low-effort tactics for establishing consistent and sensitive investigative techniques for detecting Fe in the environment. Potentiometric detectors, atomic fluorescence spectrometry, absorption spectrometry, and coupled plasma mass spectrometry are among the traditional analytical methods for Fe3+ detection (Balaram, 2021). These analytical techniques are sensitive to Fe3+ detection, but they need relatively expensive equipment, additional time, toxic chemicals, and extensive sample preparation. Compared to traditional analytical methods, nanomaterial-based fluorescence and calorimetric sensing have gained wide acceptance due to their high sensitivity, short reaction time, simplicity, low cost, and efficiency (Chandraker et al., 2019). Nanoparticles have distinct optical, electrical, and magnetic characteristics based on their core materials and a significant surface-to-volume ratio favouring miniaturization. Furthermore, the characteristics of nanomaterials are influenced by their size and form, as well as their chemical environment. Surface modification tools and technologies can also create nanoparticles with various chemical ligands and big biomacromolecules. These features have enabled researchers to develop unique diagnostic systems with considerable improvements in sensitivity, selectivity, reliability, and practicability (Agasti et al., 2010).

Fluorescent assay-based detectors are now thought to be the best technique for identifying heavy metals due to their excellent sensitivity, selectivity, low interferences, cost-efficiency, and quick response (Krishnaiah et al., 2022). Fluorescence quantum dots such as CdSe and CdTe are toxic, requiring very hazardous precursors, multiple manufacturing and purification methods, and expensive chemicals for surface functionalization. (Petryayeva et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2022; M. P. et al., 2022). As a result, it is critical to devise an innovative, simple, quick, and cost-effective strategy for creating compatible fluorescent nanoparticles (Ajith et al., 2021; Perumal et al., 2022).

Many colourimetric sensors for the selective detection of different chemicals have been proposed based on the reported changes in the colour and UV–vis absorption spectrum. “Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) and silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) based colourimetric sensors have recently generated a lot of consideration in the chemical, biological, and environmental analysis due to their easiness, cost efficiency, and great sensitivity” (Oliveira et al., 2015). The size of the NPs and the space between them define the hue of dispersion of Au NPs or Ag NPs. Consequently, the hue of the well-dispersed Ag NPs or Au NPs varies dramatically from that of the severely aggregated NPs. The capping agent regulates the surface chemistry of the NPs in all of these sensors, which is critical for the selectivity and sensitivity of the method. “Several colourimetric sensors based on Ag NPs and Au NPs capped with different chemicals, such as glutathione, folic acid, and l-cysteine, have been devised in this regard for the selective measurement of metal ions in varied water samples of metal ions in diverse water samples” (Kiatkumjorn et al., 2014; Su et al., 2014).

“Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials with particle sizes less than 10 nm that have piqued the interest of researchers due to their intriguing characteristics such as desirable biocompatibility, outstanding photostability, low toxicity, high water solubility, easy preparation, high sensitivity and selectivity to target analytes” (Ajith and Rajamani, 2021; M. P. et al., 2022). Coating of the Ag NPs changes the surface chemistry and potentially modulates the bioactivity, depending on the coating material (Akter et al., 2018). Many reports show the toxicity of Ag NPs and the non-toxicity of CDs (Ferdous and Nemmar, 2020; Ajith and Rajamani, 2021). So, the coating of Ag NPs with CDs may reduce the toxicity of AgNPs. In the present work, we are coating the Ag NPs with biogenic CDs synthesized from Ficus religiosa L. to fabricate a stable nanohybrid, Ag@CDs. We combined CDs' exceptional selective metal detection capability with the ease of use and sensitivity of the Ag NPs-based colourimetric approach. The Ag@CDs synthesized is employed to design a calorimetric and fluorescence-based multi-mode selective sensor for aqueous Fe3+ in the actual environmental water samples. Further, we used this chemosensory probe for constructing an advanced biocomputing logic system, IMP-OR gate, by employing additional inputs - ascorbic acid and urea.

The Ficus religiosa leaves were collected from the Jawaharlal Nehru University campus, India. Chemicals used for the experiment are procured from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals are used without any further purification process. Standard stock solutions of analytes were prepared in deionized water and stored at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal synthesis method was used to synthesize bio-inspired carbon dots (CDs) from F. religiosa tender leaf aqueous extract. The approach we adopted here is a slight modification of our laboratory-reported CDs synthesis procedure from F. benghalensis (Ajith et al., 2020). In brief, an aqueous homogenate of the F. religiosa L. is prepared and filtered using filter paper. 10 ml of the homogenate is taken in a 250 ml conical flask and transferred to an 800 W microwave oven system for 3 min. The synthesized CDs are cooled to room temperature and dispersed in 10 ml of deionized water. CDs are sonicated, filtered using a 0.1 μm syringe filter and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C. For fluorescence-based sensing, CDs require significant photoluminescence (PL) intensity. From a comparative study undertaken in our laboratory on the PL intensity of CDs synthesized from several bio-inspired carbon sources, we chose F. religiosa L. extracts as the carbonization precursor in this study.

Ag NPs were prepared using sodium borohydride (NaBH4) as the reducing agent and AgNO3 as the precursor (Mavani and Shah, 2013). After the complete drop-wise addition of 2 ml 0.01 M AgNO3, the NaBH4 solution turns light yellow. This indicates the formation of Ag NPs.

Ag@CDs were synthesized by modifying the ice-cold NaBH4 reduction method (Mavani and Shah, 2013). Here instead of NaBH4, CDs were used as the reducing agent. In brief, 10 ml of CDs were taken in a 250 ml conical flask; a magnetic stirrer bead was added and placed the flask in an ice bath on a string plate. CDs were stirred and cooled for 15 min. Then 2 ml of 0.01 M of AgNO3 was added drop-wise (1 drop per second). Stirring was stopped when the colour of the CDs changed from yellowish-brown to dark brown. The formed Ag@CDs were filtered using a 0.1 μm syringe filter and stored in the refrigerator.

UV–visible absorption spectra of Ag@CDs were obtained using a UV-1800 UV/Visible Scanning Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at 300–600 nm. A single beam Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) was used to capture photoluminescence at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm. By employing the Photochor dynamic light scattering instrument, the hydrodynamic radius (Rh) was measured at = 90° and wavelength 647 nm. The Stokes-Einstein equation was used to compute Rh. Zetazizer device (Malvern, UK) made zeta potential measurements between negative and positive 200 mV potential. The functional groups distributed on Ag@CDs surfaces were analyzed with Agilent Cary 630 FTIR in the 400–4000 cm−1 range. JEOL 2100 F TEM instrument running at 200 kV was used to image the Ag@CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and logic gate construction
ce:section.ce:para: In deionized water, stock solutions (1000 ppm) of metal salts – CdCl2, NiCl2, FeCl3, FeCl2, CrCl3, MnCl2, and PbCl2 and organic analytes – glucose and urea were prepared. To nullify the effect of pH on the fluorescence, Ag@CDs were dispersed in Tris-HCl buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7) to perform sensing experiments. 25 μl of Ag@CDs was diluted to 3 mL with buffer and treated with a respective analytes solution such that the final concentration of analyte in the reaction system was 100 ppm. Using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra, the binding efficiency and selectivity of interaction were investigated. The excitation wavelength was 320 nm, and fluorescence emission spectra were scanned in the 350–600 nm range.

Additionally, sensitivity analysis in a concentration-dependent way was carried out by treating Ag@CDs with progressively increasing concentrations of Fe3+ in both deionized water and natural environmental systems such as tap water and Yamuna river water (collected from Kalindi Kunj, Delhi, India). To eliminate any suspended particles, the samples were filtered through a 0.45-μm filter before being analyzed. The samples were then analyzed using the suggested sensor and the standard addition procedure, which spiked the sample with 1–100 ppm of standard Fe3+. The shift in absorbance and photoluminescence is recorded in each case. Both the selectivity and sensitivity studies are measured trice. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the fluorescence in the treatment of Fe3+ with tap water and Yamuna river water is investigated with reference to deionized water treatment to explore the feasibility of detecting Fe3+ from environmental samples. The RSD is computed by multiplying the standard deviation of fluorescence by 100 and dividing it by the mean fluorescence (Chaudhary et al., 2020).

Also, Ag@CDs is used as a probe to construct an advanced logic operation system by using additional inputs, glucose and urea. Relative fluorescence (RF) of input combinations is calculated as the ratio of obtained fluorescence with input (F) and control fluorescence without input (F0). Here the threshold value is set as the RF of 0.3. All RF above 0.3 is considered as “ON” (output = 1) and all RF below 0.3 is considered as “OFF” (output = 0).

The hydrothermal method assisted by microwave irradiation was used to synthesize CDs. The change in colour of the homogenate from green to yellowish-brown indicated the formation of CDs (Fig. 1 a. Top left inset). Carbonization of the precursor, F. religiosa L, resulted in the formation of CDs. The synthesized CDs didn't show any characteristic peaks. Still, they were highly fluorescent, whereas the synthesized Ag NPs showed a plasmon absorption peak at 390 nm with light yellow in deionized water dispersed form (Fig. 1 a. Top left inset). To synthesize Ag@CDs nanohybrid, CDs are used as the reducing agent to reduce AgNo3. “After adding complete 2 ml of 0.01 M of AgNO3 in an ice bath, the colour of CDs dispersion changed from yellowish-brown to dark brown (Fig. 1 a. Top left inset). This preliminarily indicates the formation of Ag@CDs (Beiraghi and Najibi-Gehraz, 2017). Mere cold treatment of AgNO3 will not form Ag NPs. Here CDs reduce Ag+ and create metallic silver (Ag0) (Wiley et al., 2005). c Thus, the formed Ag@CDs were highly dispersive in deionized water. Many previous reports state CDs' reducing and stabilizing potential in creating metallic NPs, including Ag NPs (Jin et al., 2015).

CDs synthesized from F. religiosa L extract are evident in the TEM studies as minute black spherical dots. HRTEM analysis revealed no distinctive lattice structures in CDs, indicating that the CDs are amorphous. According to previous research, the majority of the synthesized CDs have a low crystallinity (Hassan et al., 2017). The zeta potential was −20 mV, indicating that CDs are relatively stable. Furthermore, the computed mean hydrodynamic radius (Rh) was 2.4 nm, which agrees with the TEM data. The structure of CDs is consistent with our previously reported work of microwave-assisted CDs synthesis from F. benghalensis L extract. (Ajith et al., 2020). The detailed characterization is given in our previous study (Ajith et al., 2020).

Fig. 1 a. shows that pristine Ag NPs have a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption band at 387 nm, whereas Ag@CDs exhibit the typical SPR absorption band of Ag NPs at 416 nm. The bathochromic shift in the SPR peak indicated that the CDs had been effectively conjugated to the Ag NPs. Furthermore, the widening of the SPR peak in Ag@CDs can be explained by an increase in the size of the Ag NPs due to their conjugation with CDs (Beiraghi and Najibi-Gehraz, 2017). The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum of Ag@CDs is determined by examining emission at 320 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 b. The PLE spectrum of Ag@CDs shows an emission peak in the 350–600 nm range.

The particles' zeta potential examination revealed that the Ag@CDs were negatively charged, with an average zeta potential of 6.5 (Table 1 ). These particles produce a stable dispersion in an aqueous solution due to their negative surface charge. The particles' hydrodynamic radius (Rh) revealed that the Ag NPs had an average Rh value of 12 nm (Fig. 1 b.). The Ag@CDs' Rh value was 21 nm (Fig. 1 d.). The high Rh value is because it comprises the solvation layer when dispersed in an aqueous media. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to analyze the size and shape of the pristine Ag NPs and Ag@CDs NPs. The average particle size of the pristine Ag NPs was 9 nm (Fig. 1 c.) and the average diameter of the Ag@CDs was 16 nm (Fig. 1 e.) Fortunately, the TEM image shows that the Ag NPs persisted homogeneous and well-dispersed after being modified with CDs, which is ideal for an NPs-based fluorescence sensor. As observed in the HR-TEM image Fig. 1 f, a crystalline area with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.261 nm and an amorphous region can be distinguished in Ag@CDs. The crystalline area corresponds to the Ag NPs core, and the outer amorphous region indicates the CDs layer surrounding Ag NPs.

To study the surface functional group changes, we did FTIR analysis for Ag NPs and Ag@CDs (Fig. 1g). Absorption bands obtained from the FTIR spectrum were comparatively analyzed from the standard IR spectra table and the previous studies (Beiraghi and Najibi-Gehraz, 2017; Ajith et al., 2020; Al-Ghamdi and Mahmoud, 2014). FTIR spectrum of Ag NPs shows absorption bands at 3770, 2987, 2900, 1640, 1394, 1245, 1061 and 650 cm−1. The bands in the spectra at 3770 cm−1 correspond to O–H stretching vibrations, showing that alcohol and phenol are present. Bands originating from C–H stretching were found at 2987 and 2900 cm−1. The band at 1640 cm−1 in the spectra matches C–N and C–C stretching. The band at 1394 cm−1 demonstrates the nitro compound's NO symmetry stretching. The band shows the N–H stretching of amines at 1245 cm−1. The C–N (amines) stretch vibration was ascribed to the bands at 1061 cm−1. C–Br stretching, common in alkyl halides, might explain the band at 650 cm−1. As several studies have shown, these functional groups play a role in the stability of Ag NPs (Hoang et al., 2020; Akter et al., 2018). The FTIR spectrum of the Ag@CDs NPs showed absorption bands at 1070, 1077, 1175, 1272, 1339, 1459, 1573, 1748, 2163, 2857, 2927, 2959, and 3650 cm−1.1070, 1077, and 1175 cm−1 band correspond to C–N stretch. 1272 and 1339 cm−1 bands correspond to –CH2X (C–H wag). 1459, 1573, 1748, 2163, 2857, 2927, 2959, and 3650 cm−1 bands correspond to COO-, N–O asymmetric stretch, CO stretch, CC stretch, C–H stretch, H– N–H, C–H and O–H stretch, respectively. The result shows that the CDs-coated Ag NPs have plenty of COO-, –OH, and –NH2 groups. These functional groups can have interactions with metal ions. It also suggests that the CDs have formed a layer around Ag NPs and imparted their functional groups to the surface of Ag@CDs (Beiraghi and Najibi-Gehraz, 2017). As observed, the TEM images agree with the DLS data, FTIR analysis, and UV–vis spectroscopy measurements, demonstrating the successful creation of Ag@CDs with bigger sizes than pristine Ag NPs.

The effectiveness of Ag@CDs in binding heavy metal cations and other analytes was determined using the fluorescence spectra of Ag@CDs treated with corresponding analytes. Among analytes, Ag@CDs have a strong affinity for Fe3+ cations. The hue of the solution altered to yellowish-brown aggregated clumps after Fe3+ treatment; however, the other analyte treatment did not exhibit any colour change. (Fig. 2 b). This provides a calorimetric indication in the detection of Fe3+. When compared to other analytes, Fe3+ had a much greater rate of fluorescence quenching (Fig. 2 a). At the same concentration (100 ppm), no change in the other tested analytes was found. When the change in fluorescence intensity was plotted as a correlation graph, it was clear that the Fe3+ test solution had the lowest fluorescence intensity compared to the other analytes, implying that as-synthesized Ag@CDs are selective to Fe3+ (Fig. 2 c).

Ag@CDs were fabricated as a dark-brown dispersion that was stable and well-dispersed. The CDs surface of Ag@CDs is composed of a carbon skeleton that was edge- and surface-functionalized with a significant amount of oxygen-containing (–OH and –COO-) and nitrogen-containing (-NH2) groups that may interact well with Fe3+. The –NH2 groups were expected to connect to the silver nanoparticles' surfaces, while the –OH and –COO- groups were expected to stabilize Ag@CDs in an aqueous medium via repulsive electrostatic forces (Beiraghi and Najibi-Gehraz, 2017). This anticipation is from the FTIR analysis and the selectivity of Ag@CDs with Fe3+, not with Fe2+ and Cr3+. The –OH groups on the surface of the Ag@CDs can react with Fe3+ to create Fe(OH)3 (iron hydroxide), also formulated as FeO(OH) (ferrihydrite) (Grundl and Delwiche, 1993). Fe2+ and Cr3+ did not show significant interaction with Ag@CDs and quenching. This suggests that the electronegativity difference between Fe3+ and –OH groups on the surface of the Ag@CDs at neural pH may be the reason behind selective interaction. According to a previous study, Fe3+-induced CDs quenching is attributable to static quenching (Zhu et al., 2017). In this case, Fe3+ forms a ground-state complex with Ag@CDs before excitation. The resulting complex has its characteristics, such as unique fluorescence and adsorption. Here Ag@CDs, after cross-linking through Fe3+ ions, aggregate and form a bathochromic and hypochromic modification of the SPR absorption band from 415 to 555 nm and a colour change of Ag@CDs dispersion from dark-brown to yellowish-brown aggregated clumps. The DLS analysis further poove the cross-linking of Fe3+ with Ag@CDs. Fig. 2 e. depicts the mechanism of the Fe3+ sensor that has been fabricated.

The Ag@CDs were treated with gradually increasing amounts of Fe3+, and the absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded to test the assay's sensitivity. The UV–Visible absorption spectra (in the range of 0–100 ppm Fe3+ concentration) revealed a bathochromic change of the SPR peak location from 415 to 455 nm (Fig. 3 a). The fluorescence emission spectrum of Ag@CDs after treatment of various concentrations of Fe3+ shows that Fe3+ has a quenching effect on Ag@CDs' fluorescence, with the emission peak strength decreasing as the concentration of Fe3+ increases, i.e., exhibiting a hypochromic shift (Fig. 3 b). This shows that as-prepared Ag@CDs can be employed as probes for detecting Fe3+ via fluorescence turn-off. The quenched fluorescence intensity showed good linearity with an R2 of 0.954 with Fe3+ concentration. The calibration curve for Fe3+ detection is shown in Fig. 3 c, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.76 ppm (13.61 μM) and strong linearity as Fe3+ concentration increases. The LOD was calculated using the equation 3SD/m (SD is the standard deviation of the corrected blank signals of the Ag@CDs, and m is the slope of the calibration curve). The excellent detection range is within the Fe3+ permitted limit in both drinking and industrial water. Table 2 compare the developed probe with previously reported nano-sensors for Fe3+ detection..

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH in fluorescence of Ag@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence of Ag@CDs changed under different pH settings 2–13. At pH 6.7, the fluorescence intensity of Ag@CDs reaches its maximum. However, at pH levels below and above 6.7, the intensity of Ag@CDs fluorescence decreased (Beiraghi and Najibi-Gehraz, 2017). These findings can be attributed to carboxylate and amino group protonation under acidic circumstances (Wang et al., 2019). The dissociation of CDs from Ag NPs and the aggregation of bare Ag NPs is caused by the protonation of the amino groups. Protonation of carboxylate groups at the Ag@CDs surface, on the other hand, reduces the electrostatic repulsion between the NPs, reinforcing the self-aggregation of CDs-Ag NPs. Depending on the findings, pH levels approaching neutral were determined to be suitable for detecting Fe3+ utilizing Ag@CDs as a fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Ag@CDs in real environmental samples as sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ analysis in actual water samples was conducted using Ag@CDs to test the usability of the proposed sensor. With an average relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.48%, the produced Ag@CDs have demonstrated their incredible effectiveness in recovering Fe3+ from tap water. The RSD value for Yamuna river water is 15.78% (Fig. 4 b.). This indicates that, despite the impact of interfering groups in Yamuna water on Fe3+ detection, the proposed multi-mode sensor is an ideal nano-probe that might be employed for accurate and precise Fe3+ detection in realistic environmental water samples.

CDs and their conjugates-based logic gates are superior nano-probes that can interact with many analytes, including metal cations, anions, amino acids, pesticides, antioxidants, and other chemicals. Fluorescence tools are used in most of these logic gates because they are cost-effective, provide a quick response, and are extremely sensitive (Pawar et al., 2021). Modern science may benefit from computations based on molecular logic. In the existence of Fe3+, Urea, and ascorbic acid (AA), the fluorescence switching behaviour of Ag@CDs can be used as multiple molecular logic gates to operate Boolean algebraic logic functions. Fe3+ is used as a single input signal to construct a basic single input molecular logic gate called NOT. Fe3+ and AA can be used as inputs 1 and 2 to create a multiple-input logic gate IMPLICATION. The presence of Fe3+ or AA is given a value of 1, and their absence is given a value of 0. The highest fluorescence was assigned a value of 1, and the matching quenched fluorescence was assigned a value of 0. Only in the existence of Fe3+ and the absence of AA, i.e., input (1, 0), is considerable fluorescence quenching detected, resulting in an output of 0, but in the case of other inputs (0/0, 0/1, 1/1), the output stays 1. Further, we integrated this IMPLICATION system with a third input, “urea”, and constructed an advanced IMPLICATION-OR logic gate. Fig. 5 shows the logic symbol, truth table, and output relative fluorescence intensity at 320 nm excitation.

In the presence of Fe3+ ion in pure aqueous media, the fluorescence of the Ag@CDs was selectively quenched. As previously stated, the mechanistic investigation discovered that quenching happens due to a complex formation of Ag@CDs with Fe3+ (Grundl and Delwiche, 1993). Ascorbic acid (AA) can dissociate the Ag@CDs-Fe3+ complex formed, allowing the fluorescence of the Ag@CDs to be regained. Ag@CDs-based on-off-on probe for Fe3+ ion and AA sensing in an aqueous medium was created based on the fluorescence recovery by the AA. Further treatment with urea resulted in little or no change in fluorescence, indicating that urea has negligible interaction with Ag@CDs. This Ag@CDs-based logic system can be employed in next-generation logic-gate-based analytical applications and the detection and categorization of specific molecules in various complicated domains.

In conclusion, Ag@CDs were synthesized utilizing a fast, low-cost method that used an aqueous extract of F. religiosa L. as both carbon source, reducing, and stabilizing agent. Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission was seen in the 350–600 nm range for Ag@CDs. The synthesized Ag@CDs were ultra-selective for Fe3+, sensing it even in the presence of probable interfering chemicals seen in actual environmental samples. With the treatment of Fe3+, changes in the SPR peak and fluorescence quenching of Ag@CDs were detected as well as the hue of the solution changed to yellowish-brown aggregated clumps. The quenched fluorescence intensity showed good linearity with an R2 of 0.954 in relation to Fe3+ concentration. We also used Ag@CDs as a probe to build IMPLICATION-OR, a sophisticated logic system. It may be concluded that as-synthesized Ag@CDs can offer new paths in heavy metal detection by functioning as multi-mode sensors and logic systems due to their facile synthesis, excellent selectivity, and quick reaction time.

M. P. Ajith – Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Investigation, Data Curation, Writing Original Draft, Writing Review and Editing, Visualization.; Sonali Pardhiya– Formal analysis, Validation, Writing- Reviews and Editing, Visualization.; Arun Kumar Prabhakar - Validation, Visualization, Resources, Writing- Reviews and Editing; Paulraj Rajamani – Supervision, Conceptualization, Validation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thankfully financial assistance is acknowledged for the INSPIRE Senior Research Fellowship from the Department of Science and Technology, New Delhi, India, to AMP. We also thankfully acknowledge Mr Mohamed Nabeel Mattath from the School of Chemical Science & Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, P.R China, for helping with logic gate fabrication.
